title: The Lost Little Koala
blurb: A baby koala gets lost in a storm and must be brave to find her way home.
paragraphs: Kira was a small koala who lived high in a tall gum tree with her mother. Every morning they munched on sweet eucalyptus leaves and watched the sun rise over the bush. Kira loved her home. She knew every branch and every knot in the bark.

One windy afternoon, dark clouds rolled across the sky. The wind howled through the leaves. Kira held on tight, but a strong gust shook the branch and she tumbled down, down, down into the wet bushes below.
Rain poured down. Kira huddled under a fern until the storm passed. When she peeked out, everything looked strange and wet. Every gum tree looked the same. She could not see her mother anywhere.
"Stay calm," she told herself. "I can do this." Her heart was beating fast, but she took a deep breath and stood up on her shaky legs.
Kira sniffed the air. She remembered that her tree smelled of sweet eucalyptus leaves — a smell like home. Slowly, she followed the scent, climbing over wet logs and around muddy puddles.
A kookaburra laughed from a branch above. A wallaby hopped past and gave her a curious look. Kira kept going, even when she felt tired and scared.
The sun began to set, painting the sky orange and pink. Just when Kira thought she might never find her way, she heard a soft call. "Kira! Kira!"
It was her mother! Kira scrambled up the trunk as fast as she could and hugged her tight. "I was so worried," said her mother. "You were very brave."
That night, Kira fell asleep curled up safely in the fork of the tree. She was not scared anymore. She knew she was stronger than she thought.
wonder: How do you think Kira felt when she could not see her mother?
What clever idea helped Kira find her way home?

title: Bilby and the Big Race
blurb: A little bilby learns that trying your best matters more than winning.
paragraphs: Every year, the desert animals held a big race across the red sand. Kangaroos, emus, and lizards all lined up at the start. This year, Benny the bilby decided to join in.
His friends giggled when they saw him. "Your legs are too short!" said a lizard. "You'll never keep up," said a kangaroo. Benny's ears drooped, but he did not give up.
"I'll try my best," he said quietly. From that day on, Benny trained every morning. He ran past the spiky spinifex grass and over the hot dunes until his legs grew strong.
Race day arrived. The sun blazed overhead and a crowd of animals gathered to watch. "On your marks, get set, go!" cried the old goanna starter.
The emu shot ahead, kicking up clouds of red dust. The kangaroo bounded along beside it. Benny ran as fast as his little legs could carry him, but he fell further and further behind.
By the time Benny reached the finish line, the race was over. He was last. He was puffed, sweaty, and his paws were sore. Some animals laughed. Benny felt his whiskers droop.
Then Benny noticed something near the finish line. A young echidna was stuck in a hole, wiggling and calling for help. While the winners celebrated, Benny stopped and gently pulled the echidna free.
"You did not win the race," said the echidna gratefully, "but you have the biggest heart of all." The watching animals went quiet, then began to clap.
Benny smiled. That felt even better than first place. He had learned that being kind matters more than being fast.
wonder: Why did Benny's friends think he could not race?
What did Benny do that showed he was kind?

title: The Kangaroo Who Couldn't Hop
blurb: Joey the kangaroo wants to hop like the others, but his legs just won't bounce.
paragraphs: Joey was a young kangaroo who lived in a wide green paddock with his mob. Every afternoon, the other joeys would bounce and race across the grass, leaping high into the air.
But Joey had a big problem. Every time he tried to hop, he just flopped over. His back legs felt heavy and clumsy. The other joeys giggled, and Joey hid behind a tree.
"Maybe I will never hop," Joey sighed that evening. His mum nuzzled him gently. "Keep trying, little one. Strong legs take time. Even I was wobbly once."
The next morning, Joey decided to practise in secret. He wobbled. He fell. He bumped his tail. He scraped his paws. But he got up every time and tried again.
Day after day, Joey practised at dawn before the others woke up. Slowly, his back legs grew stronger and springier. He could feel the difference, even if nobody else could see it yet.
One sunny morning, Joey crouched low, swished his tail, and pushed hard with both feet. Boing! He flew into the air and landed neatly on the grass. He had done it!
"I did it!" Joey shouted. He hopped once, twice, three times — higher each time. A magpie on the fence watched and sang a cheerful song.
When the other joeys came out to play, Joey joined them. He bounced across the paddock, grinning from ear to ear. He was the happiest kangaroo in the whole mob.
His mum watched from the shade and smiled. Joey had learned that if you keep trying, you can do things you never thought possible.
wonder: What did Joey's mum tell him when he felt sad?
How did practising every day help Joey?

title: Stripes the Curious Quokka
blurb: A quokka on a sunny island discovers what is hiding behind the big rock.
paragraphs: On a warm island near the sea lived a quokka named Stripes. He had a friendly face and a bouncy hop. Stripes loved to explore. He always wanted to know what was around the next corner.
Every day he visited the beach, the bush, and the rocky cliffs. His friends were happy to stay in one place, but Stripes could never sit still for long.
One morning, Stripes spotted a huge rock he had never been behind. It stood at the far end of the island, half hidden by scrub. "I wonder what is back there," he whispered.
His friend Wally the wallaby shook his head. "Probably nothing special," he said. But Stripes could not stop wondering.
He hopped over driftwood and squeezed past prickly bushes. Thorns caught his fur, but he kept going. The rock grew bigger and bigger as he got closer.
When Stripes finally rounded the corner, he gasped. Behind the rock was a hidden pool of clear blue water, sparkling in the sun like a jewel.
Tiny crabs danced on the sand at the water's edge. Bright fish flashed below the surface. A small waterfall trickled down the rock into the pool. Stripes had found a secret place all his own.
He sat on the warm stone and dipped his paws in the cool water. It was the most beautiful place he had ever seen.
From then on, Stripes visited the pool every day. Sometimes he brought Wally, who agreed it was very special indeed. Being curious had led Stripes to something wonderful.
wonder: What does the word 'curious' mean?
What did Stripes find behind the big rock?

title: The Great Beach Cleanup
blurb: When Maya finds rubbish on her favourite beach, she gathers her friends to help.
paragraphs: Maya loved going to the beach near her home in Melbourne. She went every weekend with her family. She loved the salty air, the squawking seagulls, and the cool waves washing over her toes.
She knew the names of the shells she found and the best spot to build sandcastles. The beach felt like a second home.
But one Saturday, Maya saw something that made her frown. The sand was covered in plastic bottles, bags, and bits of old rope. The tide had washed up more rubbish than she had ever seen.
Near the water's edge, a little turtle was tangled in a piece of fishing net. It was struggling to swim free. Maya's heart sank.
Carefully, Maya waded into the shallow water and gently freed the turtle. It looked at her with dark, round eyes, then paddled safely back into the sea. Maya felt relieved, but also angry.
"This beach needs help," she told her mum on the drive home. "We have to do something."
Maya spoke to her teacher on Monday. Together they made a plan. The next weekend, Maya brought her whole class to the beach. They wore gloves and carried big bags.
For two hours they picked up every piece of rubbish they could find. Bottles, wrappers, old thongs, and tangled rope all went into recycling bins. By lunchtime, the beach was clean again.

An old fisherman walked by and smiled. "You children have done a wonderful thing," he said. "The fish and the birds will thank you." Maya felt proud. Looking after nature was everybody's job — and it had started with her.
wonder: Why was Maya upset when she got to the beach?
What can you do to help look after the place where you live?

title: Mila's First Day at School
blurb: Mila has butterflies in his tummy on his first day at a new school.
paragraphs: Mila's family had just moved to a new suburb. Everything was different — a new house, a new street, and soon a new school. Mila had never been the new kid before.
On Monday morning, he stared at his lunch box and his new school shoes. His tummy felt full of fluttering butterflies. "What if I have no friends?" he asked. "What if nobody talks to me?"
His dad knelt down and gave him a hug. "Just be yourself, Mila. Smile and say hello. That's how friendships begin."
At the school gate, Mila held his dad's hand tightly. The playground was noisy and full of children he did not know. He wanted to hide behind his dad forever.
In the classroom, Mila sat quietly in the corner and looked at his desk. The teacher smiled at him, but Mila was too nervous to smile back.
At recess, Mila stood alone near the handball courts. Then a girl with a bright purple headband walked over. "Hi! I'm Priya," she said. "Do you want to play handball with us?"
Mila hesitated for a moment. Then he grinned and nodded. Soon he was laughing and racing around the playground with Priya and her friends.
They played handball, then tag, then sat on the grass sharing stories about their pets. Mila told them about his cat, Whiskers, and everyone laughed at the funny things Whiskers did.
When his dad picked him up at the end of the day, Mila couldn't stop talking. "I love my new school!" he said. The butterflies were all gone.
wonder: Why did Mila feel nervous about his first day?
How did Priya help Mila feel better?

title: The Magic Boomerang
blurb: Jack finds an old boomerang that always returns — even from impossible places.
paragraphs: Jack loved visiting his grandfather's farm on school holidays. There was always something new to explore — old sheds, paddocks full of sheep, and hills that stretched to the horizon.

One rainy afternoon, while exploring the dusty shed behind the farmhouse, Jack found something wonderful. Tucked behind a stack of tools was a beautiful old boomerang covered in painted patterns of dots and lines.
"That belonged to my grandfather," said Grandpa, peering over Jack's shoulder. "Take it outside and give it a throw." He winked. "See what happens."
Jack went to the open field behind the house. The rain had stopped and the sun was breaking through the clouds. He held the boomerang carefully and swung his arm.
The boomerang curved through the air in a wide, graceful arc and came spinning right back into his hand. Jack's mouth fell open. "Wow!"
He threw it again, harder this time. The boomerang sailed over the fence, looped around a hill, and still found its way home. Jack ran and threw it again and again.
He tried throwing it left, right, high, and low. No matter how far or which direction he threw it, the boomerang always returned. It was as if it could never get lost.
Jack's little sister Lily came outside to watch. "Let me try!" she said. The boomerang came back to her too, and they both laughed with delight.

That evening, sitting by the fire, Grandpa explained that the boomerang was very old and had been made with great care and skill long ago. "Some things," he said softly, "are made to always come back to you." Jack held it close, feeling lucky to be its keeper.
wonder: What was special about the boomerang Jack found?
Why do you think the boomerang was important to Jack's grandfather?

title: The Night the Stars Fell
blurb: Two friends camp in the backyard and witness an amazing shower of shooting stars.
paragraphs: Ava and Sam had been best friends since prep. They did everything together — riding bikes, building Lego, and making up silly songs. This Saturday was special: their first backyard campout.
They dragged sleeping bags and pillows into the tent their dads had set up. Mum brought hot chocolate in a thermos and extra blankets. "Don't stay up too late," she said with a smile.
When the house lights went out, Ava and Sam lay on their backs and stared up through the mesh roof into the darkness. The sky was enormous, dotted with thousands of tiny lights.
"Look!" gasped Ava suddenly. A bright streak of light shot across the sky and vanished. Then another, and another. The stars seemed to be falling!
"They're shooting stars," Sam whispered, remembering what his dad had told him. "It's called a meteor shower. Quick, make a wish!" They both squeezed their eyes shut and wished as hard as they could.
For an hour, they lay still and watched. They counted the streaks — ten, twenty, fifty! Some were quick flashes; others left glowing trails that faded slowly. The sky put on a show just for them.
Sam set up his small telescope and they took turns looking at the moon's craters and a bright planet low on the horizon. Ava drew the shooting stars in her notebook by torchlight.
Eventually their eyelids grew heavy. They snuggled into their sleeping bags, still whispering about the amazing sky.
When they finally fell asleep, they were still smiling. It was a night they would remember forever — the night the stars fell.
wonder: What was really happening when the stars seemed to fall?
If you saw a shooting star, what would you wish for?

title: Ruby and the Rainbow Lorikeet
blurb: Ruby helps an injured lorikeet and gains a colourful little friend.
paragraphs: Ruby loved spending time in her garden. She watered the flowers, chased butterflies, and listened to the birds singing in the trees. Her favourite tree was the big bottlebrush, with its bright red flowers.
One sunny afternoon, Ruby heard a sad little squawk. She looked around and found a rainbow lorikeet sitting on the ground under the bottlebrush. Its wing was hanging at a strange angle.
The bird tried to hop away but could not fly. Ruby knew she had to help. Gently, she scooped the bird into a soft towel. "Don't worry," she whispered. "I'll look after you."
Ruby's mum called the vet, and they drove there straight away. The vet was kind and careful. She wrapped the lorikeet's wing and explained how to feed it special food and keep it warm.

At home, Ruby made a cosy box with a towel and a small dish of water. Every day she fed the bird and talked to it softly. Slowly, the lorikeet began to perk up. It would tilt its head and chirp when Ruby came near.
After two weeks, the vet said the wing was strong enough to try flying. Ruby carried the box outside to the garden. Her hands trembled a little as she opened the lid.
The lorikeet stretched its wings, flapped once, twice — then soared up into the blue sky. Ruby watched until it was just a colourful speck among the clouds.
She felt happy and sad at the same time. She would miss her little friend. But the next morning, a familiar squawk came from her window. The lorikeet was back, sitting on the sill as if to say hello.
From that day on, it visited every morning. Ruby had made a friend for life by being kind.
wonder: What was wrong with the lorikeet when Ruby found it?
How did Ruby show that she was a caring person?

title: The Cubby House Club
blurb: Four friends build the best cubby house ever — but only by working together.
paragraphs: Tom, Lily, Hassan, and Grace were best friends who lived on the same street. One hot summer holidays, they decided to build a cubby house in Tom's big backyard.
On the first morning, they gathered under the old oak tree with a pile of wood, some nails, and big dreams. But trouble started right away.
"It should have a flag on top!" said Tom. "No, a secret door!" said Lily. "We need a roof that won't leak," said Hassan. Grace wanted a window to let in light.
Everyone talked at once. Nobody listened. By lunchtime, they had argued so much that not a single piece of wood had been nailed. Tom kicked at the dirt and sighed.
Then Grace had a thought. "Why don't we use ALL our ideas?" she said. "Tom can make the flag. Lily can build the secret door. Hassan is good at hammering, and I'll draw the plan."
The others looked at each other and nodded. It was a brilliant idea. Grace drew a plan on a piece of cardboard. Hassan measured the wood. Tom and Lily carried the heavy planks together.
With everyone helping, the cubby house slowly came together. Tom painted a flag bright red. Lily fitted a hidden door that only they knew about. Hassan hammered the walls until they were sturdy.
By sunset, the cubby had a flag, a secret door, sturdy walls, and a little window that let in golden light. They even added a string of fairy lights for sleepovers.
The four friends crawled inside and grinned at each other. "This is the best cubby in the world," said Hassan. "And we built it as a team." They all agreed.
wonder: Why couldn't the friends build anything at the start?
How did working as a team help them succeed?

title: The Brave Little Firetruck
blurb: Pip is the smallest firetruck in the station, but one day she saves the day.
paragraphs: Pip was the smallest firetruck in the whole fire station. She was bright red with a shiny silver ladder, but she was much shorter than the big engines beside her.
When the alarm rang, the big trucks zoomed off with their sirens wailing. Blaze and Thunder raced to every fire in the city. Pip was usually left behind in the station.
"You're too little for real jobs," the big trucks would say. Pip felt sad, but she never gave up. Every morning she polished her ladder and checked her hose, ready for anything.
The firefighters liked Pip. They knew she was eager and brave, even if she was small. "One day, Pip," said Firefighter Ana, "your size will be just right for something important."
One hot afternoon, the alarm rang again. Smoke was rising from the old market district. Blaze and Thunder roared out of the station — but Pip stayed behind once more.
Then a second call came in. A fire had started in a narrow laneway between two tall buildings. The big trucks raced there, but they were too wide to fit through the tiny gap.
"This is a job for Pip!" said the fire chief. Pip's engine rumbled to life. She squeezed through the narrow lane, her mirrors scraping the brick walls.
At the end of the lane, a rubbish bin was on fire. Pip aimed her hose and sprayed water until the flames were out. The crowd cheered. The big trucks arrived just in time to see Pip save the day.
From that day on, nobody ever called Pip 'too little' again. Being small had made her just the right size for the job.
wonder: Why was Pip usually left behind at the station?
How did being small help Pip save the day?

title: Grandpa's Veggie Patch
blurb: Ella learns that good things grow for those who are patient.
paragraphs: Every Sunday, Ella visited her grandpa's house. Her favourite thing to do was help him in his veggie patch at the bottom of the garden.
Grandpa had been growing vegetables for fifty years. He knew exactly when to plant, when to water, and when to pick. Ella loved learning from him.
One spring morning, they planted tiny carrot and tomato seeds in the soft brown soil. Ella patted the earth gently and imagined the vegetables already growing.
"When can we eat them?" she asked the very next day. Grandpa chuckled and wiped his hands on his apron. "Not yet, little one. Plants need time, water, and sunshine."
Ella frowned. Waiting was hard. But she came back every Sunday to water the seeds with her little green watering can. She pulled out weeds and watched the empty patch hopefully.
Some days she thought nothing was happening at all. "Are they growing, Grandpa?" she asked. "Under the soil, yes," he said. "You just can't see them yet."
Then one morning, tiny green shoots poked up through the dirt. Ella squealed with delight. Week by week they grew taller, their leaves spreading wide.
By summer, bright red tomatoes hung from the vines and orange carrots peeked above the soil. Ella and Grandpa picked a big basket full together.
Ella bit into a sweet, warm tomato and grinned. Juice ran down her chin. "It was worth the wait," she said. Grandpa nodded. "The best things often are."
wonder: What three things did Grandpa say plants need to grow?
What does it mean to be patient?

title: The Mystery of the Missing Cricket Ball
blurb: When the cricket ball vanishes, young detective Sam follows the clues.
paragraphs: It was a hot summer day and Sam's backyard was the perfect cricket pitch. The stumps were an old bin and a chair. The boundary was the back fence.
Sam, Jake, and Priya had been playing for an hour. The score was close. Sam was batting, and Jake was bowling. The whole neighbourhood could hear them cheering.
Sam swung hard. Crack! The ball flew high over the fence and into the bushes near the shed. "Six!" shouted Priya. But when they ran to look, the ball was gone.
They searched under the bushes, behind the shed, and in the garden beds. Nothing. The bright red ball had completely vanished. "This is a mystery," said Sam seriously.
Sam decided to become a detective. He fetched his magnifying glass and notebook. "Every mystery has clues," he told his friends. "We just have to find them."
He looked carefully at the muddy ground near the fence. There, pressed into the soft earth, were paw prints. They were too big for a cat and too round for a bird.
Sam followed the prints across the lawn, under the trampoline, around the compost bin, and behind the shed. The trail was clear. Jake and Priya followed, whispering excitedly.
The prints stopped at a wooden doghouse. Sam knelt down and peered inside. Curled up fast asleep, with the cricket ball tucked safely between his paws, was Rusty the dog.
Everyone burst out laughing. Rusty opened one eye and wagged his tail, still holding the ball. Sam the detective had solved the case. Rusty got his very own ball to keep, and the game continued.
wonder: What clue helped Sam find the missing ball?
Why do you think Rusty took the cricket ball?

title: Echidna's Spiky Problem
blurb: Eddie the echidna wishes he wasn't so spiky — until his spikes save him.
paragraphs: Eddie the echidna lived in a cosy burrow at the edge of the bush. He was covered in sharp, brown spines from his nose to his tail. When he walked, he made a soft rustling sound.
Eddie wished he had soft fur like the wallabies, or smooth skin like the lizards. "My spikes make it hard to hug my friends," he sighed. "Everyone is careful around me."
He watched the other animals play together, rolling in the dust and bumping into each other. Eddie stayed at the edge, too worried about poking someone.
One afternoon, Eddie was searching for ants among the fallen leaves. He did not notice the hungry goanna creeping towards him, its forked tongue flicking in and out.
Eddie looked up just in time. The goanna lunged! Quick as a flash, Eddie curled up into a tight, prickly ball. His spines stuck out in every direction.
The goanna tried to grab him but only got a nose full of sharp spikes. "Ouch!" it cried, and scrambled away into the bush. Eddie stayed curled up until his heart stopped pounding.
Slowly, he uncurled and peeked out. His spikes had kept him safe. For the first time, he felt glad to have them.
The next day, Eddie told his friend Wally what had happened. "Your spikes saved you," said Wally. "I think they're brilliant." Eddie felt a warm glow inside.
From that day, Eddie felt proud of his spines. "They might be prickly," he said, "but they are exactly what makes me, me."
wonder: Why did Eddie wish he was different at the start?
How did Eddie's spikes turn out to be a good thing?

title: A Trip to the Great Barrier Reef
blurb: Noah goes snorkelling and discovers a magical underwater world.
paragraphs: Noah had been counting down the days until the family holiday. They were going to Queensland to visit the Great Barrier Reef — the largest coral reef in the whole world.
After a long drive and a bumpy boat ride, Noah pulled on his mask and snorkel. The water was warm and so clear he could see the sandy bottom far below.
He floated face-down and let the current carry him gently. Below him stretched a world of colour he had never imagined. Coral grew in shapes like brains, fans, antlers, and mushrooms.
Fish in every colour darted in and out of the reef — blue, yellow, orange, and purple. Some were tiny; others were as long as Noah's arm. They moved like a living rainbow.
A green sea turtle glided past, flapping its flippers slowly like wings. Noah held his breath in wonder. The turtle looked ancient and wise, as if it had swum these waters for a hundred years.
His mum tapped his shoulder and pointed. Hidden in a waving sea anemone was a bright orange clownfish, just like in the movies. Noah waved at it, and it peeked back at him with one curious eye.
They snorkelled for an hour, drifting over gardens of coral and schools of shimmering fish. Noah's dad showed him a giant clam with a mouth like a purple flower.
Back on the boat, Noah couldn't stop smiling. His fingers were wrinkly from the water, but he didn't care. "The reef is like a city under the sea," he said. "Full of animals and colour and life."
His mum nodded. "And we have to look after it," she said, "so it stays beautiful for everyone." Noah agreed. He would never forget what he had seen beneath the waves.
wonder: Name two animals Noah saw under the water.
Why did Noah say we have to look after the reef?

title: The Dancing Cockatoo
blurb: Coco the cockatoo loves to dance, even when others think it's silly.
paragraphs: Coco was a white sulphur-crested cockatoo who lived in the city park. She had a bright yellow crest and a loud, cheerful squawk. She loved the park, especially on weekends when music filled the air.
Every Saturday, buskers came to play guitars and drums near the fountain. Whenever music played, Coco could not help herself. She bobbed her head, lifted her feet, and danced.
The other birds in the park thought it was silly. "Birds are supposed to sing, not dance," squawked a pigeon. "You look ridiculous," said a crow, fluttering away.
Coco felt embarrassed for a moment. She stopped dancing and tucked her crest flat. But then the music started again — a lively tune with a bouncy beat.
Coco could not resist. She raised her crest high, lifted her feet, and swayed to the rhythm. She spun and bobbed and tapped her claws on the pavement.
A little girl pointed and giggled with delight. Then more people stopped to watch. Soon a crowd had gathered, clapping and smiling at the dancing cockatoo.
They threw coins into the busker's hat and cheered for Coco. The busker laughed and played faster. Coco danced even harder, her crest bouncing with every step.
The other birds watched from the trees in surprise. The pigeon tilted its head. The crow stayed to watch. Maybe being different wasn't so silly after all.
Coco just grinned and kept on dancing. She knew that being yourself was the best thing of all.
wonder: Why did the other birds think Coco was silly?
What happened when Coco kept dancing anyway?

title: Lily and the Lighthouse
blurb: In a wild storm, Lily helps her grandmother keep the lighthouse shining.
paragraphs: Lily was staying with her grandmother for the school holidays. Grandma lived in an old lighthouse on a rocky point by the sea, where the wind blew salty and strong.
Every evening, Grandma climbed the winding stairs to light the great lamp. At night, the beam spun round and round, warning ships to stay away from the dangerous rocks below.
Lily loved helping. She polished the brass fittings and coiled the ropes neatly. Grandma taught her the names of the ships that passed on the horizon.
One evening, dark clouds gathered over the ocean. The wind began to howl, and waves crashed against the cliffs with a thunderous roar.
Rain lashed the lighthouse windows. Lily and Grandma sat by the fire, listening to the storm. Then, with a sudden flicker, the great lamp went out. The lighthouse was dark.
"The bulb has blown," said Grandma, her face worried. "There's a ship out there in the dark. We must fix it quickly, or it could hit the rocks."
Grandma fetched a new bulb and a torch. Together they climbed the steep, winding stairs. The wind shook the tower, but they held the railing tight and kept climbing.
At the top, Grandma unscrewed the old bulb while Lily held the torch steady. Her hands were shaking, but she did not drop it. Grandma fitted the new bulb, and the great light blazed back to life.
Far out at sea, a ship's captain spotted the beam just in time. He steered safely past the rocks into calm water. Lily hugged Grandma tightly. She had helped save the day, high up in the lighthouse.
wonder: What is a lighthouse used for?
How did Lily and her grandmother work together in the storm?

title: The Footy Final
blurb: Zoe's team is losing the grand final, but the final whistle teaches her something.
paragraphs: Zoe had played footy since she was five years old. She loved everything about it — the muddy boots, the team songs, and the thrill of chasing the ball across the oval.
This year, her team, the Magpies, had won every game. Now they had made it all the way to the grand final. The whole school was coming to watch.
On the morning of the big day, Zoe's tummy flipped with excitement and nerves. She pulled on her black and white guernsey and laced up her boots twice.
The other team, the Tigers, were fast and strong. They had won the flag last year. When the siren sounded to start the game, both teams ran hard.
By half-time, the Tigers were ahead. By the last quarter, the Magpies were behind by two goals. Zoe's legs ached and her cheeks burned red, but she kept running.
In the final minute, the ball bounced free near the goals. Zoe sprinted, scooped it up, and kicked with all her strength. The ball sailed through the middle posts. A goal! The crowd roared.
But when the final siren sounded, the Tigers had still won by a single point. Zoe sank to her knees. All that effort, and they had lost.
Then the Tigers' captain walked over and shook her hand firmly. "You played a great game," she said. "That was the best goal all day." Zoe looked up, surprised.
Zoe smiled and shook hands with every player on both teams. They had lost the match, but she had learned something important: how you play matters just as much as whether you win.
wonder: How did the Tigers' captain show good sportsmanship?
What did Zoe learn from losing the grand final?

title: The Friendly Dragon of Dandenong
blurb: The townsfolk are afraid of the dragon on the mountain — until they meet him.
paragraphs: High in the misty Dandenong hills, where tree ferns grew tall and lyrebirds sang, lived a dragon named Doug. He was large and green with gentle golden eyes.
The people in the town below had never met Doug. They only saw smoke rising from his cave on cold mornings. "A dragon must be dangerous!" they whispered to each other. "We should stay away."
But Doug was actually very gentle. He spent his days reading old books, growing ferns in pots, and baking rock cakes on hot stones. He was kind — and very lonely.
Sometimes Doug looked down at the twinkling lights of the town and wished someone would visit. But nobody ever climbed the steep, winding path to his cave.
One cold winter, the town ran out of firewood. The people huddled in their coats, shivering. The shops closed early. Even the school was chilly.
A brave girl named Mei remembered the stories about the dragon. "Maybe he can help," she told her mother. Her mother looked worried, but Mei was determined.
Mei packed a bag with an apple pie and climbed the mountain alone. The path was steep and slippery, but she kept going. When she reached the cave, Doug peeked out nervously.
To her surprise, he smiled kindly. "I made too many rock cakes," he said. "Would you like one?" Mei told him about the cold town. Doug nodded and blew a warm, gentle breath down the hillside.

The town's lanterns and fireplaces flickered to life. Everyone grew warm and cosy. After that, the townsfolk visited Doug often. They had learned an important lesson: you should never judge someone before you get to know them.
wonder: Why were the townsfolk afraid of Doug at first?
What lesson did the town learn about the dragon?

title: Possum on the Roof
blurb: A noisy possum keeps the family awake, so they find a clever, kind solution.
paragraphs: Oliver's family had just moved into a cosy weatherboard house with a big gum tree in the backyard. They loved everything about their new home — except one thing.
Every night, thump, thump, thump! A brushtail possum ran across the roof. It sounded like someone was bowling a cricket ball above their bedrooms.
Oliver's little sister couldn't sleep. His parents were tired and grumpy at breakfast. Even the cat stared up at the ceiling with annoyed eyes.
"Can't we make it go away?" Oliver asked. His mum shook her head firmly. "We can't hurt the possum. It was here before us. We need a clever idea."
That afternoon, Oliver sat under the gum tree and thought. He watched the possum climb down at dusk, its bushy tail curled behind it. It was actually quite cute.
Then Oliver had an idea. "What if we build the possum its own home in the garden?" he said at dinner. "A possum box, like the ones at the wildlife shelter."
The family looked at each other and nodded. On Saturday, they built a cosy wooden box together. Oliver hammered nails while his dad sawed the wood. His sister painted flowers on the side.
They fixed the box high in the gum tree, safe from cats and dogs. Inside, they placed a piece of apple and some soft leaves as a welcome gift.
That night, the possum found its new home and snuggled in. The roof was finally quiet. Oliver smiled in the darkness. Everyone was happy — especially the possum.
wonder: Why didn't the family want to hurt the possum?
What clever solution did Oliver come up with?
